md: Two dashboards in every car tell the same story from opposite ends.
**Speedometer**: how fast you're going *right now*. An *instantaneous rate*.
**Odometer**: how far you've gone *in total*. An *accumulation*.
These look like different quantities — but they're inseparably linked. Press Play.
### Two graphs, one motion
**Top graph**: speed $v(t)$ at every instant. **Bottom graph**: cumulative distance $s(t)$.
Watch closely as the car drives. Two things happen simultaneously:
**Shaded area under $v(t)$** accumulates and equals $s(t)$ at every moment.
**Red slope of $s(t)$** at the current point equals the speedometer reading $v(t)$.
These two observations *are* the two halves of the **Fundamental Theorem of Calculus**.

blocks.title: FTC, in human language
blocks.body:
Part 1 — **derivative recovers speed**: if $s(t) = \int_0^t v(\tau)\,d\tau$, then $\dfrac{ds}{dt} = v(t)$.

Part 2 — **integral recovers distance**: $\int_a^b v(t)\,dt = s(b) - s(a)$.

Differentiation and integration are inverse operations. The car has been demonstrating this on every trip you've ever taken.

md: ### Why area = distance

Imagine the speed is constant at $v = 18\text{ m/s}$ for $10$ seconds. Distance is $v \cdot t = 180\text{ m}$. That's the **rectangle** under the (flat) speed graph: width $10$, height $18$, area $180$.

What if speed varies? Slice time into tiny pieces of width $\Delta t$. In each tiny slice, speed is roughly constant at $v(t_i)$, so distance traveled in that slice is $v(t_i)\,\Delta t$ — a thin rectangle's area. Sum all the rectangles: that's the area under the curve. As $\Delta t \to 0$, that sum becomes the integral $\int v\,dt$. **Total distance is the area under the speed curve.** Try the trip presets above to see this for non-constant speeds.
### Why slope = speed

Now look at the *distance* curve. Speed is the rate of change of distance — how many meters per second you're piling up at this instant. That's exactly the slope of the distance-vs-time graph at the current $t$. The red tangent line on the bottom plot shows it.
When the car is parked: distance curve is flat → slope $= 0$ → speedometer reads $0$.
When the car is accelerating: distance curve is curving upward (slope increasing) → speedometer reading rises.
When the car is braking: distance curve keeps climbing but the slope is decreasing → speedometer reading falls.

blocks.title: Try this
blocks.body: Switch to **"Stop & go"**. During the stops, the distance graph is flat — exactly because the speedometer reads zero. Switch to **"Accel then brake"**. The fastest distance accumulation happens at $t = 5$, exactly where the speedometer is at its peak.

md: ### From cars to all of calculus
Anywhere you have a *rate* and an *accumulation*, the FTC applies the same way:
**Water flow rate (L/s) ↔ tank volume (L)**: the volume curve's slope is the flow rate; the flow rate curve's area is the volume change.
**Population birth rate ↔ total population growth**.
**Economic income rate ↔ total wealth**.
**Heat flow rate ↔ total heat absorbed**.
Once you grasp the speed-distance pair, derivatives and integrals stop feeling like two separate ideas. They are one idea seen from two directions.
